The issuer’s call option beginning August 15, 2026, at par provides a hard floor at $25, insulating investors from downside if market yields compress. Should Chimera refinance at lower rates or retire high‑cost debt, note holders realize full principal plus accrued interest.

Chimera is Growing Stronger

Investing in such a note carries credit risk, the risk that Chimera will not be able to repay its obligations. It is important to note, that the notes carry, as we said, a BBB investment grade rating. Furthermore, in the last five years, Chimera has grown net income available to common shareholders from $15.1 million in 2020 to $125.25 million in the trailing twelve months (TTM). In the Q1 2025 earnings call, Phil Kardis notes that, “Earnings available for distribution improved by 11%, our book value increased by 7.4%, and our economic return was 9.2%”, going on to suggest that the Palisades acquisition had been successfully and quickly integrated. With improving profitability comes decreasing risk with respect to the notes. This is because greater profits mean that Chimera’s capacity to meet its obligations rises. By way of example, Kadis explained that,

“This quarter, we exercised our call rights on all our non-Remic securitizations and issued 2 new securitizations backed by those loans. This was effectively a cashout refinancing that unlocked $187 million at a reinvestment hurdle below 6%, and there's more.”

This demonstrates management’s proactive balance sheet management, driving down future funding costs and preserving excess liquidity. The company’s proactive strategy enhances Chimera’s ability to service the CIMO notes even under widening spreads. Although Chimera’s debt structure includes both recourse and non-recourse leverage, they are both fairly low and manageable, with GAAP recourse leverage at 1.2:1 and total GAAP leverage at 3.09:1 as of Q1 2025.

Another risk is that interest rates rise such that they put pressure on Chimera's profitability. At present, there is no indication of aggressive interest rate hikes, and this is unlikely given the economic certainties and mix of inflationary pressures and recession risks. Market volatility in the broader financial markets could also impact the trading price of CIMO. However, as a senior unsecured debt, CIMO's price is generally less volatile than the underlying common stock. Since its issuance in 2024, the price has been fairly stable.

The Right Time to Buy

At $24.97, the notes are trading slightly below par value, giving investors free optionality should yield compress or Chimera call them at $25 in August 2026. I believe that this is the right time to buy, as the investor can maximize yield and minimize potential capital loss if the notes are called in 2026. Investors who buy at above par run the risk that if the notes are called in August 2026, they would face a capital loss. Buying at above par may be acceptable if investors are willing to take on that risk.

Risks

Before investors take the leap, investors need to weigh various company and bond-specific risks. First, if a sustained uptick in mortgage spreads occurs or there is Fed policy tightening, this could compress Chimera’s net interest spread, pressuring earnings available for distribution. Second, a sudden contraction in the non-Agency RMBS or securitized loan markets could widen Chimera’s asset-liability spread beyond what its hedging strategy can cover, risking NAV drawdowns and rating pressure. In addition, although these baby bonds are more liquid than most securitizations, a spike in credit-spread volatility or a market-wide sell-off could widen bid-ask spreads, delaying execution or forcing steep markdowns. In the Q2 1025 earnings call, management addressed this, with CIO Jack McDowell assuring analysts that, "We have built additional liquidity and positioned our portfolio to withstand volatility, spread widening, and funding shocks if they were to emerge."

Conclusion

Chimera’s CIMO notes offer an attractive, high single-digit yield, have an investment grade rating, a call-at-par floor, and a sub-par price offering optionality. Given, the firm is growing more profitable and will therefore be more able to meet its obligations, this is happening as the company already has an attractive debt profile. I strongly recommend buying these notes.

data.title: Intuitive Machines: Success After Lunar Failure
data.description:
Intuitive Machines (NASDAQ:LUNR) surprised Wall Street with positive free cash flow during in Q1 2025. The stock has been rewarded with a 22.4% gain, but still trades well below its highs from January. In March, I reiterated my strong buy rating for LUNR stock and that has worked out rather well with a 24% gain so far.

As I discuss in this report, the company has a lot of opportunities but also a lot of trust to recover after two moon missions that did not go smoothly, which is a soft way to put it.

LUNR Fixing The Moon Mission

Intuitive Machines stock lost altitude after its second moon landing mission, IM-2, ended up landing on its side in March, ending the mission early. After the first mission, which saw the lander tipping over, expectations were high for success on the second mission, but it ended in an utter disappointment. The stock price plunged on the news in a market environment that already signalled lower risk appetite.

The company has addressed three points that required improvement to assure a precise and soft landing. The first is laser altimeter interference, the second being lighting conditions, and the third is crater recognition. For the third mission, the company has added additional altimeters to its sensor package, added a lighting independent sensor for sensing surface velocity and the crater database has been enhanced.

So, there are a lot of useful changes that were made to increase the chances of success for the third mission, scheduled for later this year. The reality remains that the company is booking progress, but the IM-2 was underwhelming.

During the Q1 2025 earnings call, CEO Steve Altemus said he believes IM-3 will be a success:

Moving forward, we will succeed, land softly, land upright, land ready to operate. We've identified the issues and are making the necessary changes we believe will get us there on IM-3.

While I do believe the sensor suite has been significantly enhanced to increase chances of mission success, I do not put a lot of credence to the CEO’s statement on mission success. The reason for that is that management’s response in the wake of the disappointing IM-2 was not strong.

Intuitive Machines Has Major Opportunities Ahead

Intuitive Machines believes that more work and contracts being outsourced to the private sector creates opportunities for the likes of Intuitive Machines. I would not disagree with that assessment, but do note that when it comes to the track record for the moon missions IM does not have an outstanding one and the reality is that outsourcing in some cases, just like with the commercial lunar payload services happens at a fixed-price basis. We do not know whether the sixth contract that is expected to be awarded later this year has the same contract set up. What we do know is that fixed-price contracts transfer all risks to the contractor.

That does not mean that there are no opportunities ahead. The company has two CLPS awards that it competes for this year that could be meaningful if won. The first one is CS6, the sixth payload delivery mission, and the second one is from the Science Mission Directorate. Both are part of the Commercial Lunar Payload Services program.

Towards the end of the year the LTV or Lunar Terrain Vehicle contract will be awarded if the program is set to continue and additional task orders for NSNS to set up a relay network are also to be expected with Task Order 2 valued $18 million being awarded in April. The company is also working on a nuclear-powered satellite, which is of national security interested given that it would mean that solar panels are no longer required, making the satellites less observable. The company is also working adding Earth re-entry capability, which could come in handy for some space objectives including microgravity research where the experiment has to be returned to Earth after experiments have been conducted.

Another opportunity is the OSAM part of OMES III. Intuitive Machines won the Omnibus Multidisciplinary Engineering Services III, which provided a significant and consistent revenue stream, but NASA ended up cancelling the OSAM part of the contract. OSAM stands for On-orbit Servicing, Assembly and Manufacturing. Intuitive Machines is now looking at whether it can apply OSAM to defense customers to revive that revenue stream.

Fourth Quarter Loss Widens Despite Revenue Growth

While Intuitive Machines is in growth mode, its revenues are not. First quarter revenues decreased $10.7 million. The decision from NASA to cancel the OSAM project within the OMES III contract drove a $20.1 million decrease in revenues for that contract while the CLPS mission contracts revenues declined $1.5 million partially offset by higher lunar terrain vehicle revenues of $6.9 million and NSN in the amount of $3 million while other engineering services increased $1 million.

The CLPS decline is driven by the fact that the company could recognize $12.3 million in restricted revenue for IM-1 in the same period last year, but the IM-2 restricted revenues are slipping into Q2 with half of the $14.4 million expected in Q2. The decrease in IM-1 revenues was partially offset by revenues for IM-2 through IM-4.

Cost of revenues decreased by $3.4 million driven by the lower revenue base, resulting in a $10.1 million operating loss. Pre-tax income rose from a $118 million loss to a $1 million profit driven by lower loss on securities and a positive impact of the fair value of the warrants that the company redeemed, partially offset by higher earn out liabilities.

Driven by timing of receipts, the company saw a $13.3 million free cash flow compared to $8 million a year ago, ending the quarter with $373.3 million in cash driven by proceeds of $176.6 million from warrants exercised.

The positive is that the company no longer has significant pressure to raise capital as it also secured a $40 million liquidity line and all warrants are exercised. Which should bode well for investors as the company eyes positive run rate EBITDA by the end of the year and positive EBITDA for 2026.

What Is Intuitive Machines Stock Worth?

For Intuitive Machines, I am using a P/S valuation method using revenue estimates for the years ahead. Against its own median price-to-sales ratio, the stock is not worth much and also, when we look at the industry, LUNR stock appears overvalued. However, I do believe that Intuitive Machines is the kind of company that has significant potential and should be valued in line with Rocket Lab, which I consider the gold standard in space opportunities.

By doing so and applying the minimum P/S of 6.1x for RKLB to LUNR, we get price targets of $14.70 for 2025 and $20 for 2026. So, I am maintaining my strong buy rating for now.

Conclusion: Intuitive Machines Needs To Deliver

The first quarter results showed a decline in revenues driven by the IM-2 constrained revenues slipping into Q2 and lower OMES III contract revenues. That is disappointing. However, we do see higher LTV and NSN revenues flowing into the system and, overall, Intuitive Machines has the opportunity to decouple its revenue growth from payload delivery services where it has yet to meet expectations. We also keep in mind any pivot away from lunar exploration in favor of targeting visits to Mars by the current administration, but do note that lunar exploration is an ideal springboard to expand to exploration of Mars. A positive during the quarter was the positive free cash flow, but this was largely driven by the timing of receipts.

While we keep in mind the risk of the IM-3 mission, I do believe that the company remains a strong buy as it has key contract awards ahead and task orders to delivery on, with new task orders in the pipeline.

data.title: SPY: Expected Economic Aftershocks Make Me A Bear Right Now
data.description:
U.S. stock markets, as commonly represented by the SPDR S&P 500 Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY), have rallied substantially from the trade policy hurricane that hit in early April. While there was incredible uncertainty in capital markets a month ago, and even famed investor Steve Eisman argued that investors should adopt a "Don't Be A Hero" mentality, equity indices have climbed back to levels even higher than when the Trump Administration's “Liberation Day” tariff announcements sent the market reeling.

I'm not terribly surprised by the recovery, for a plurality of reasons:

  • No matter his recent insistence to the contrary, I never doubted the importance that President Trump places on stock market performance. He promoted strong stock market performance during his first term as U.S. President, for example. Eventually, his administration was going to be swayed by market stress.
  • Punitive tariffs on important trading partners were naturally going to impact the everyday lives of Americans eventually, and major retailers' warning of “empty shelves” in the near term hurried the need for a tariff resolution.
  • Retail investors have had 1.5 decades of proof (in their minds) that market pullbacks are always worth buying, "no questions asked". I didn't expect them to abandon that approach without experiencing significant personal economic pain (which hasn't hit—April's strong jobs report makes that clear).

Monday's (April 12) heroics following the announcement of a thawing of U.S.-China trade tensions (including a 90-day pause of reciprocal tariffs) have brought the S&P 500 within earshot of YTD gains. Three-quarters of the S&P's 20% decline from February 19th's all-time high has now been recovered.

The remainder of this article examines and predicts where U.S. stock markets may go from here.

Revisiting Beginning-Of-Year Expectations

Despite having been bearish on U.S. stock markets in recent years, and the fact that I've been actively hedging away equity upside exposure for income, in late-December 2024, I presented an upside outlook for the S&P 500 for this year. In fact, I was predicting about a 10%-12% upside on top of the ~57.5% total return that the SPY has offered investors for the two-year period between January 1, 2023, and January 1, 2025.

In "2025 Markets To Be Driven By Liquidity, Lobbying And Collectibility - Most Likely", I predicted a year-end S&P 500 Index value of 6,440.

S&P 500 Earnings Estimates

Coming into 2025, Wall Street Analysts were expecting S&P 500 earnings to clock in at $250.66 for the year (see above-linked article for details). At the time, those forecasts represented nearly a full 20% increase in earnings as compared to 2024.

Here is how I viewed the catalysts as the calendar turned:

Tailwinds for 2025 earnings include:

  • AI efficiency gains, as various industries apply artificial intelligence solutions;
  • Lower interest rates (than last year);
  • Lower corporate tax rates (potentially).

Headwinds for 2025 earnings include:

  • Strong USD (DXY) economics, as U.S. products become more expensive, and as transnationals' international earnings translate to less in USD terms;
  • Supply chain issues due to a possible implementation of tariffs;
  • Strain on U.S. consumers from higher prices due to tariffs;
  • The cost side of implementing AI solutions (it's a big investment);
  • Already record-high corporate margins.

-Source: my December 20, 2024 article.

Here's an interesting question for readers to ponder: Given all that has transpired during the first ~4 months of 2025, how do you think S&P 500 estimates have changed? Per Ycharts, current expectations stand at $238.44, which represents about a -4.9% decline.

On this basis, the index was recently trading at a 24.1x multiple. That remains at the all-time highest levels in history, excluding recessionary periods.

While U.S. GDP did fall -0.3% for Q1 2025, the economy isn't (yet) formally in recession. Nonetheless, some investors may reasonably argue that 2025 earnings will be temporarily depressed and that 2026 will be a period of greater corporate normalcy. That seems to play out in the forward earnings estimates of $274.19 for the S&P 500 next year. If current expectations are realized, earnings will grow +13.5% this year, and +15% in 2026. Next year's estimates would equate to a forward P/E of just under 21x at recent S&P 500 levels.

Why Now Might Be A Good Time To Worry

When I presented my 2025 S&P forecast at the end of last year, I was readily aware of the possibility that both higher consumer prices and supply chain strains from tariffs could pose meaningful challenges to Corporate America.

Attempting to put myself back into my own shoes from late-December 2024, I think current (effective) U.S. import tariffs on foreign goods would be within my band of expectations. What I would never have guessed, however, was the path that has taken us to this point, including 100%+ tariffs on Chinese goods entering the United States for about a month. I also wouldn't have expected the smorgasbord of temporarily high reciprocal tariffs on a variety of other countries for a period of time.

Officially (at least based on what has been made public), those ex-China reciprocal tariffs are on a 90-day pause ending in early July, and the China reciprocal tariffs (beyond 30%, which is still significant) are on pause for 90 days effective this week.

Generally speaking, I do not believe that these punitive reciprocal tariffs will be reactivated after those 90-day pauses. I think the capital markets drama of the past ~6 weeks, and an upcoming economic fallout, to make the Trump Administration a bit more gun-shy. With that said....

  • Inevitable and highly public declarations that the Trump Administration has lost the trade war will likely result in the U.S. President making additional threats against foreign countries. I strongly expect this and that it will cause further economic instability across various lands, including the United States.
  • I think some investors believe that the revocation of high reciprocal tariffs suddenly puts us in the clear. I don't view it that way at all. A highly integrated global economy has experienced supply-chain dislocations whose effects have now hit the main street economy, in my opinion. I expect the tariff ordeal to resemble an earthquake with aftershocks, and those aftershocks could have meaningful consequences on confidence and investment.

Why Unhedged Foreign ETFs like VFV:CA Look Particularly Vulnerable

If I'm turning bearish on the S&P 500 now, I'm especially bearish on foreign tracking ETFs that benefit from a strong U.S. Dollar. We've already seen strong evidence of capital flows moving to places other than the United States. Even if the tariff drama is on the backburner, I think global confidence in the world's most powerful country has been injured for years to come.

Furthermore, while the Trump Administration's objective was to repatriate capital investment dollars back to the United States, businesses may now be reluctant to commit to investing in the country due to policy unpredictability.

Tracking ETFs like Vanguard's VFV:CA have outperformed the S&P 500 in recent years, and the main reason for that has been the depreciating Canadian Dollar.

Meanwhile, currency-hedged ETFs like Vanguard's U.S. Total Market Index ETF (VUS:CA) have underperformed in recent years.

I'm expecting the switch to flip, and underpinning an appreciating Canadian Dollar (which is my expectation) may be suddenly patriotic Canadians' decisions to limit their spending south of the border (on either products or travel). Canadian businesses may opt out of renewing supply contracts with American exporters when they come due, and I don't expect lost tourism dollars to find their way States-bound again anytime soon.

Conclusion And Recommendation

After clawing back all of their losses since “Liberty Day”, major U.S. stock indices seem at risk here, especially if aftershocks from the trade wars hit the economy in the next few months.

Meanwhile, the U.S. Dollar looks rich as investment capital seems destined to continue to look for a new home away from the unpredictability of the United States.

I'm converting to a bearish view on broad U.S. markets, and am especially pessimistic about foreign-based ETFs that track the S&P 500 without currency hedges.

data.title: A Pair Trade Opportunity By Bank of America's Preferred Stocks
data.description:
Co-authored by Relative Value.

Overview

The US tariff announcement in early April 2025 stirred almost all market sectors and product types. Currently, the general market, represented by the S&P 500 index, has recovered from the high-volume selling that the tariff uncertainty caused and is priced above its values from before the announcement. This is not the case with the fixed-income market, especially the long-term securities. The selling pressure in the longest-term treasuries caused equalizing in the call options' values within a group of similar long-duration fixed-income products with the same issuer, effectively making them trade with very close yields. The deviation from the long-term yield spread between the same types of securities gives us a profit opportunity when these spreads mean-revert. With this short article, we'll present such a deviation from Bank of America Corporation's (NYSE:BAC) listed perpetual preferred stocks - the 6.00% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series GG, Bank of America Corporation 6 NCUM PFD SR GG (NYSE:BAC.PR.B) and the 4.125% Depositary Shares Non-Cumulative Preferred Stock Series PP, Bank of America Corporation 4.125% DP PFD PP (NYSE:BAC.PR.P) and take a look at the potential expected profit.

Bank of America Overview

Bank of America, a Delaware corporation, headquartered in Charlotte, North Carolina, is a leading financial holding company on a global scale and the second-largest bank by asset size in the US. The company provides a wide range of financial services in the US and international markets through its business segments: Consumer Banking, Global Wealth and Investment Management, Global Banking and Global Markets, and All Other. Bank of America is considered systemically important for the US financial system, and as such, it is included in the "Too Big to Fail" list. The price chart below shows how the general public has evaluated the common stock of the company for the past ten years:

If we compare the total return performance of Bank of America to that of the S&P 500 and SPDR® S&P Bank ETF (KBE) for the same period, we get the following chart:

Bank of America's total performance over the past ten years seems to have been moving with the market's general trend, outperforming on the upswing and underperforming on the downswing. As the general trend was up, it should come as no surprise that BAC's total return is better than the S&P 500's. The performance of the company compared to the average US bank, represented by the KBE ETF, is more impressive - Bank of America has outperformed twice the sector in the total return metric.

Regarding the future valuation of Bank of America, the SA Analysts are giving it the neutral "Hold" rating, the Quant Rating gives a bullish "Buy" score, while according to the Wall Street analysts, the company is a real bargain at this valuation and give it the "Strong Buy" rating.

If we look at the Quant Rating Factor Grades of the company and compare them to the valuations it received six months ago, we see deterioration in the Valuation and Momentum markings, and improvement in Growth and Earnings revisions. The strongest mark the company gets for its Profitability performance, as this was the worst score six months ago. The improvement is due mainly to the better TTM cash from operations of the company, compared to six months ago. The $4.55 billion of cash from operations of Bank of America is way above the average $155 million for the sector, but still far below the average value of $19.4 billion the company has managed to accomplish in the past five years.

Taking the comparison from above a step further, we can put the total return performance of Bank of America against other big diversified banks:

Bank of America's performance is second only to JPMorgan Chase & Co.'s (JPM) over the ten-year period. The two biggest US banks had almost identical total returns until the end of 2022, but since then, JPM has outperformed significantly and currently has almost twice the total return BAC has.

Company Credibility

Fitch Ratings - New York - 12 Jun 2024: Fitch Ratings has affirmed Bank of America Corporation's (BAC) Long- and Short-Term Issuer Default Rating (IDRs) at 'AA-' and 'F1+', respectively. Additionally, Fitch has affirmed Bank of America, N.A.'s (BANA) Long- and Short-Term IDRs at 'AA' and 'F1+', respectively. The Rating Outlook on the Long-Term IDRs is Stable. A full list of rating actions is below.

Strong Intrinsic Profile: BAC's intrinsic creditworthiness, as expressed by its Viability Rating (VR) of 'aa-', drives its IDRs. The VR reflects BAC's leading franchise in many of its core businesses, its diversified business mix and consistent strategy, which Fitch believes will continue to underpin a more stable through-the-cycle financial performance relative to peers. BAC's VR is one notch above the implied VR of 'a+' due to the company's business profile, which Fitch assesses at 'aa-' and considers a high influence factor in its ratings.

This is the beginning of the recent rating report with which Fitch affirms the 'AA-' long-term issuer default rating with a stable outlook for Bank of America. The rating history for the past couple of years of the company is depicted below. Bank of America's last credit upgrade was four years ago in June 2021, and since then, according to Fitch Ratings, there have been no substantial changes in the credit quality of the corporation.

Capital Structure

Bank of America recently issued an earnings report for the First Quarter of 2025, so we can see the company's actual capital structure. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is also provided for comparison purposes, so anyone interested can see how it evolved historically in the short term.

As of Q1'25, Bank of America had a total debt of $773.8 billion, ranking senior to the outstanding preferred stock equity of approximately $20.5 billion. At the lowest position in the company's capital structure is the $275.1 billion of common equity. Calculated this way, Debt-to-Equity for Bank of America amounts to 2.81 - a higher value than the average of 1.23 for the diversified banks sector in the US as of May 2025.

Dividends

Bank of America has an 11-year history of increasing common stock dividends and, even more impressive, 30 years of uninterrupted dividend payments. This is what the dividend history of the company looks like:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to BAC's shareholders amounts to $1.02 in the form of quarterly payments. With the current price of the stock at $43.36, that corresponds to a 2.35% dividend yield. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past 10 years:

And the forward estimations for the dividend and dividend yield:

Bank of America has approximately 7.8 billion common shares outstanding and a $1.04 forward estimated annual payment per share. We can calculate around $8.1 billion forward annualized distribution for the common stockholders. The total $1.5 billion annual dividend for the preferred shares of the company represents approximately 18.5% of that sum. The cushion that the common shares distributions present is large enough for the preferred shareholders to feel considerably confident that they will receive their payments in the foreseeable future.

Bank of America Bonds

Next, we want to take a look at the debt of Bank of America. The corporation currently has close to one hundred debt issues trading on the bond market.

The long-term senior debt of Bank of America is rated 'A1' by Moody's and is priced with a yield to worst of approximately 5.8%, or 90 basis points above the 20-year treasuries.

As we can see from the average risk premium for the single-A rated US corporate debt currently, Bank of America's long-term debt is priced quite in line with its peers with similar credit quality. Nothing currently indicates that the bank's creditors have any reason to demand a higher yield from the company's debt.

BAC preferred stocks pair trade

As we noted in our recent article, pair trades among the same types of securities of a common issuer are among the safest bets credit-wise anyone can make on the exchange. Due to the different valuations of the embedded call options, the securities are trading with a long-term average yield spread. From time to time, however, the up shifts in the respective treasury benchmark cause the difference in the call options evaluations to narrow and even disappear. From that point onward, on a continuous up shift in the benchmark yield, the securities tend to move together on the downside, keeping almost identical yields. On a downshift in the benchmark yield, the call options tend to return to their long-term values, so our expectancy is that the long leg in our pair trade should appreciate more in value than the short one.

Bank of America currently has ten listed outstanding preferred stocks - all of them but BAC-E are perpetuities with fixed rates of payment. In the chart below are listed the relevant metrics for the securities. The fixed-rate perpetuities with a PAR value of $25 are priced within a very tight current yield spread.

Due to the differences in their call option prices under normal market conditions, BAC-B and BAC-K usually trade with higher current yields than the rest of the fixed-rate $25 PAV value preferreds. As explained above, this valuation changes in times of intense selling in fixed-income products, when the call options of the preferred stocks in the group grow closer in value, and the CY spread disappears. The chart below shows the historical current yields of BAC-B in blue and BAC-P in red, since the IPO of BAC-P. May 2022, October 2022, and October 2023 are examples of such occasions when the call option price difference in the two securities disappears, and their current yield metrics become equal.

These were the times when the current yield spread of the two preferred stocks disappears, and they start moving together on the downside. Sooner or later, they mean-revert with BAC-P having more potential on the upside.

The reason for this, as stated above, is that when the selling pressure is over, the reason for the call options prices to be equal is gone with it, and the securities CY spread mean reverts, returning to its long-term values. This gives the trader who takes a long position in BAC-P and short in BAC-B the opportunity to try to exploit the deviation and subsequent normalization.

The chart above shows the average CY spread between BAC-B and BAC-P of 61 basis points in favor of BAC-B's metric. Currently, BAC-P's current yield is 13 basis points higher than BAC-B's, indicating a potential of 74 basis points for the pair trade. That corresponds to approximately $2.20 or over 13% of potential per share for the (BAC-P long - BAC-B short) pair trade. We have the more expensive security in the short leg, so the pair trade is effectively credit-risk free.

One of the biggest problems with the pair trades is the carry cost, so it has to be considered in all cases. Currently, BAC-B has a borrowing fee of approximately 3%, or a $0.06 cost per month of holding the short position. This is not a whole lot, looking at a $2.20 potential reward, and that the previous times the current yields of the two securities mean-reverted within a couple of months. However, as we should well know, in this line of business, there are no guarantees - neither that the borrowing fee will stay this low, nor that the pair trade's potential will be closed in the foreseeable future.

Conclusion

This is a short article, with which we want to turn your attention to the pair trade opportunity that BAC-P and BAC-B are currently offering, similar to a couple of times in the past few years. We looked into the reasons why on the upside, BAC-P has more potential than BAC-B, and on the downside, both securities should move together. Both stocks are among the most liquid preferreds on the exchange, so getting in and out of the position should not be a problem. The 13% credit risk-free potential is appealing for risk-averse investors. However, discretion is required, as the carry costs could liquidate the trade's potential.
